<tool-use data-tool-type="read" data-tool-name="Read"><details>

 `./board/archive/NOTE-0012_fix-robots-txt.md`

```text
1	---
2	type: note
3	status: done
4	created: 2026-04-01
5	---
6	
7	# Fix invalid robots.txt on nasapicture.com
8	
9	The current `robots.txt` at nasapicture.com contained the full landing page HTML appended after the valid directives. Crawlers would misparse or ignore the rules.
10	
11	## Cause
12	
13	The landing site is deployed via Cloudflare Pages (`nasapicture-landing` project). There was no `robots.txt` in `landing/`, so Pages' SPA catch-all served `index.html` for `/robots.txt`. Cloudflare's managed bot protection then prepended its directives to the HTML response — resulting in valid robots rules followed by an entire HTML document.
14	
15	## Fix
16	
17	Added `landing/robots.txt` with a simple `Allow: /`. Cloudflare's managed content (AI bot blocks, content signals) prepends cleanly to this instead of to HTML. Will take effect on next Pages deploy.
18
```
</details></tool-use>

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-6 2026-04-01 09:47:47Z)**_

140:### NOTE Lifecycle
142:NOTEs are the "to be refined" inbox. When processed, they **spawn** new artifacts:
145:NOTE (inbox) → process → spawns TASK(s), REFE(s), ADR(s), RESO(s) → archive NOTE (processed)
148:- NOTEs spawn, never convert — the original stays as historical record
149:- Never delete NOTEs — archive with `status: processed`
150:- One NOTE can spawn many artifacts, or none
177:- ADRs spawn TASKs and REFEs, never NOTEs
179:- Status is validity (active/superseded/deprecated), not workflow
193:- Status stays `backlog` (no separate "blocked" status)
199:- Tasks with `status: done` → move to `board/archive/`
200:- Resources with `status: processed` → move to `board/archive/`
201:- Notes with `status: processed` → move to `board/archive/`
216:- **IDs don't carry over** — TASK-0022 retyped to NOTE becomes NOTE-0005 (next available), not NOTE-0022

+## Security Assessment: APOD Proxy Cloudflare Worker
+
+**Risk Level: LOW**
+**Immediate Action Required: No**
+
+This is a well-scoped, read-only proxy with a small attack surface. Most findings below are hardening recommendations, not urgent vulnerabilities.
+
+---
+
+### 1. API Key Exposure
+
+**Severity: LOW** -- The NASA API key is never included in any response to the client. The `fetchApod` function builds the NASA API URL server-side (line 54-56), and the response to the user is either proxied image bytes or sanitized JSON fields (title, date, explanation, etc.). The `/info` endpoint explicitly picks which fields to return (lines 196-202), so the raw NASA response (which could theoretically contain request metadata) is not forwarded wholesale.
+
...

Yes, that's the issue. GitHub has a ~10MB limit for rendering images in READMEs, and the HD APOD can exceed that. Switch it to the SD endpoint:

_**User (2026-04-01 10:34:20Z)**_

in markdown do images always render full width?

---

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-6 2026-04-01 10:34:25Z)**_

By default in GitHub markdown, images render at their natural size (up to the container width). If the image is wider than the content area, it'll fill the width — which is likely the case with APOD images.

GitHub markdown doesn't support width attributes on `![]()`  syntax, but you can use an HTML `<img>` tag with a `width` attribute instead. For example:

```html
<img src="https://api.nasapicture.com/image-sd.jpg" alt="Today's APOD" width="600" />
```

Want me to constrain it to a specific width?

---

_**User (2026-04-01 10:37:03Z)**_

mmmm is that bad practice?

---

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-6 2026-04-01 10:37:07Z)**_

Not really — HTML in GitHub READMEs is common and well-supported. But it's a judgment call. The full-width image is arguably fine here since it's the hero/showcase of the project. A massive space photo filling the width is kind of the point.

Up to you — leave it full width, or constrain it?
